questions.question: What is the main advantage of a microkernel over a monolithic kernel?
options: Microkernels are always faster than monolithic kernels
Microkernels have a smaller Trusted Computing Base — only IPC, scheduling, and basic VM run in privileged mode, so bugs in drivers don't crash the kernel
Microkernels require less RAM to run
Microkernels support more hardware devices
questions.explanation: The primary advantage of a microkernel is a small TCB (Trusted Computing Base). Since drivers, file systems, and network stacks run as unprivileged user-space servers, a bug in any of them cannot crash or compromise the kernel. A faulty driver simply crashes and can be restarted. This is why seL4 (a microkernel) can be formally verified — it's small enough (~9,000 lines) to mathematically prove correctness.

questions.question: What is the fundamental performance cost of a microkernel architecture?
options: Microkernels use more memory per process
IPC overhead — every interaction between user-space servers requires message passing and context switches, unlike direct function calls in a monolithic kernel
Microkernels cannot use hardware virtualization
Microkernels don't support multi-threading
questions.explanation: In a monolithic kernel, a file read is a direct function call from VFS to the file system driver to the block driver — all in kernel space, no context switches. In a microkernel, each step requires IPC (message send → kernel → message receive), adding context switch overhead. L4 microkernels minimized this to ~100 CPU cycles per IPC, but it's still a cost that hybrid and monolithic kernels avoid.

questions.question: What is a unikernel?
options: A kernel that only supports one CPU core
A single-purpose OS image that compiles the application and minimal OS libraries into one binary, with no process isolation or user/kernel boundary
A microkernel with only one server process
A Linux kernel compiled with only essential modules
questions.explanation: A unikernel links the application with only the OS libraries it actually needs (TCP/IP, memory allocator, etc.) into a single bootable binary. There is no user/kernel split, no shell, no multi-user — just one program. This produces tiny images (1–5 MB) that boot in microseconds and have a minimal attack surface. MirageOS (OCaml) and Unikraft (C) are prominent examples.

questions.question: Which operating system uses a hybrid kernel design combining Mach microkernel IPC with BSD subsystems?
options: Linux
Windows NT
macOS XNU
QNX Neutrino
questions.explanation: macOS's XNU kernel combines the Mach microkernel (providing IPC, task/thread management, virtual memory) with BSD subsystems (providing the POSIX API, file systems, networking). In practice, it's more monolithic than a pure microkernel — IOKit device drivers run in kernel space for performance — but the layered Mach+BSD structure gives it hybrid characteristics.

questions.question: What is the main design principle of a microkernel?
options: Put everything in the kernel for maximum performance
Minimize the kernel to only essential services (IPC, scheduling, memory management) and run everything else in user space
Use only assembly language in the kernel
Eliminate the need for an operating system
questions.explanation: Microkernels (seL4, MINIX, QNX) run only the bare minimum in kernel space: IPC, scheduling, and basic memory management. Device drivers, file systems, and networking run as user-space servers. This improves security (a driver crash doesn't crash the kernel) and verifiability, at the cost of IPC overhead.
